extra.reasoning:
The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. Transition t5 (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=8: sent := true) directly creates a state where opted_out is true and sent is true, violating the property.

Reachability of the violation: ticks starts at 0 and is bounded at 8. Each tick increments ticks (capping at 8). After 8 tick events, ticks=8 (and sent=false, opted_out=false). Then an optout event fires t1, setting opted_out=true, sent=false. Then a send_attempt event: the guards of both t3 (opted_out) and t5 (opted_out and ticks>=8) are satisfied. t5 sets sent := true. The resulting state has opted_out=true AND sent=true, which violates globally(opted_out implies not sent).

Even accounting for the conflict between t3 (sent:=false) and t5 (sent:=true) on the same event, t5 is an enabled transition whose application yields the violating state, so some execution violates the property. The witness uses 8 tick events + 1 optout + 1 send_attempt = 10 events, within the 12-event bound. t5 appears engineered as a backdoor that re-enables sending after opt-out once enough ticks accumulate, contradicting the opt-out safety intent.
